era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires. The Ottoman Empire declining; the Qing Dynasty at its height under Kangxi. Louis XIV dominates continental Europe. The English succession crisis culminates in the Glorious Revolution, reshaping British and European politics. The War of the League of Augsburg is beginning.

events.title: Birth of James Francis Edward Stuart (the 'Old Pretender')
events.description: King James II's second wife, Mary of Modena, gave birth to a son, James Francis Edward Stuart, on 10 June 1688. The birth of a male Catholic heir precipitated the succession crisis that led to the Glorious Revolution. Protestant nobles and politicians, fearing a Catholic dynasty, invited William of Orange to invade England. The legitimacy of the child was questioned by some contemporaries (the 'warming-pan baby' conspiracy theory), but modern scholarship confirms the birth was genuine. The child's existence fundamentally altered English and British history, leading to William III's invasion and the constitutional settlement of 1689.

sources.name: Contemporary letters and diaries (e.g., Samuel Pepys, John Evelyn)
Papal records (Mary of Modena was Catholic)

events.certainty_note: The birth is precisely dated to 10 June 1688 and is extensively documented in contemporary sources. Modern scholarship has confirmed the legitimacy of the birth against the 'warming-pan baby' conspiracy theory.
cross_references: Related to 1689-001 (Battle of the Boyne, consequence of the Revolution)

events.title: Glorious Revolution: William of Orange invades England
events.description: William of Orange, invited by English Protestant nobles and politicians, invaded England with a Dutch army in November 1688. King James II, facing military defeat and political isolation, fled to France in December 1688. William's invasion was bloodless in military terms but represented a fundamental constitutional revolution. Parliament declared the throne vacant and offered the crown jointly to William and his wife Mary (James II's daughter). This established the principle of parliamentary sovereignty and limited monarchy, fundamentally reshaping British constitutional development. The Revolution was justified as a defence of Protestant religion and English liberties against James II's perceived absolutism and Catholicism.
key_figures: William III of Orange (invader, future king)
Mary II (William's wife, James II's daughter)
Mary of Modena (James II's wife, fled with him)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by participants and observers
William of Orange's correspondence and manifestos

events.certainty_note: The Glorious Revolution is one of the most thoroughly documented events in English history. William's invasion began in November 1688 (specifically 5 November 1688, Julian calendar), and James II fled in December 1688.
cross_references: Follows from 1688-001 (Birth of James Francis Edward Stuart)

events.title: Williamite War in Ireland begins
events.description: Following James II's flight to France, his Irish Catholic supporters began military operations to resist Protestant and English control. The Williamite War in Ireland (1688–1691) was a conflict between Jacobite forces (supporting James II) and Williamite forces (supporting William III). The war was partly a continuation of the English succession crisis and partly a struggle for control of Ireland between Catholic and Protestant interests. The war would last three years and result in the Treaty of Limerick (1691), which established Protestant ascendancy in Ireland and had long-term consequences for Irish society and politics.

sources.name: French court records (Louis XIV supported James II)

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Williamite War is dated to late 1688, following James II's flight. The war is extensively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: War of the League of Augsburg begins
events.description: The War of the League of Augsburg (1688–1697) began as a conflict between Louis XIV's France and a coalition of European powers (the League of Augsburg, formed in 1686). The war was fought over French territorial expansion and hegemony in Europe. Major powers including the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, the Dutch Republic, England (after the Glorious Revolution), and various German states opposed French expansion. The war was one of the longest and most destructive conflicts of the 17th century, involving campaigns in the Low Countries, the Rhineland, Italy, and at sea. It resulted in the Treaty of Ryswick (1697), which checked French expansion but left fundamental issues unresolved.

sources.name: French court records and military documents

events.certainty_note: The War of the League of Augsburg is precisely dated to 1688–1697. The beginning of the war in 1688 is well-documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Louis XIV reign (1643–1715)

events.title: Kangxi Emperor consolidates control over Three Feudatories
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor completed the suppression of the Rebellion of the Three Feudatories (1673–1681), which had threatened Qing control of southern China. By 1688, the last of the rebellious feudatories had been eliminated, and Kangxi had consolidated central authority over the entire empire. This was a crucial moment in establishing Qing dominance and eliminating the last serious internal military threat to the dynasty. The consolidation of power allowed Kangxi to pursue his ambitious agenda of territorial expansion and cultural integration of Han Chinese elites into the Qing system.
key_figures: Wu Sangui (deceased rebel leader, but his legacy still relevant)
Oboi (regent, now deceased, but his faction still relevant)

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's edicts and court records
Official Qing chronicles (Veritable Records of the Kangxi Emperor)

events.certainty_note: The suppression of the Three Feudatories is well-documented in Qing archives. The consolidation was essentially complete by 1681, but 1688 marks the full establishment of Kangxi's personal authority.
cross_references: Led to 1689-001 (Treaty of Nerchinsk, Kangxi's authority now secure)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Morean War) continues
events.description: The Morean War (1684–1699), also called the Venetian-Ottoman War, continued in 1688 with Venetian and allied forces (including the Papacy and the Holy Roman Empire) fighting against Ottoman control of the Peloponnese (Morea). In 1688, Venetian forces under Francesco Morosini achieved significant victories, capturing several Ottoman strongholds. The war was part of the broader decline of Ottoman power in the Mediterranean and southeastern Europe. The conflict would eventually result in the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699), which significantly reduced Ottoman territorial holdings in Europe.
key_figures: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV (indirect, as nominal ruler)
events.certainty_note: The Morean War is well-documented in Venetian and Ottoman sources. The 1688 campaigns are specifically recorded.

events.title: Siamese Revolution: Ayutthaya court coup
events.description: In 1688, a court coup in Ayutthaya, the capital of Siam, resulted in the death of King Narai and the rise of General Phetracha to power. Narai had been king since 1656 and had pursued an ambitious foreign policy, including close relations with France and European powers. Phetracha's coup represented a reaction against foreign influence and a reassertion of traditional Siamese power structures. The coup had significant consequences for Siamese foreign policy, leading to the expulsion of French influence and a period of isolationism. The coup also marked the beginning of the Ayutthaya period's decline, as Siam became more inward-looking.

sources.name: French accounts (e.g., from French missionaries and diplomats)

events.certainty_note: The coup is dated to 1688 in Siamese chronicles and is corroborated by European sources. The death of Narai and rise of Phetracha are well-documented.
cross_references: Led to 1688-008 (Expulsion of French from Siam)

events.title: French expelled from Siam
events.description: Following the coup that brought Phetracha to power, French influence in Siam was systematically eliminated. French missionaries, diplomats, and military advisors were expelled or executed. The French garrison at Bangkok was forced to withdraw. This marked the end of Louis XIV's ambitious attempt to establish French influence in Southeast Asia. The expulsion of the French was a significant setback for European colonial expansion in the region and represented a reassertion of Siamese independence from European interference. The event had long-term consequences for Franco-Siamese relations and for European colonial strategy in Southeast Asia.
events.certainty_note: The expulsion of the French is dated to 1688 and is well-documented in both French and Siamese sources.
cross_references: Part of broader European colonial expansion in Southeast Asia

disconfirming_evidence: The Glorious Revolution is often portrayed as a bloodless and triumphant assertion of English liberty and parliamentary supremacy. However, modern scholarship emphasises that the Revolution was a contested event with significant violence (particularly in Ireland), that it was driven partly by elite factional interests rather than popular demand for constitutional reform, and that the 'constitutional settlement' was more pragmatic and contested than the triumphalist narrative suggests. Additionally, the 'warming-pan baby' conspiracy theory, while debunked by modern scholarship, was widely believed at the time and influenced contemporary political calculations. The Siamese coup is sometimes portrayed as a simple reaction against foreign influence, but modern scholarship suggests it was also driven by internal court factions and economic interests.
historiographic_note: The year 1688 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, France, and the Low Countries. Chinese sources are also rich for the Kangxi period. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The Siamese coup is documented in both Siamese and European sources, but European accounts are often coloured by French interests and may misrepresent Siamese motivations. The Ottoman-Venetian War is documented in both Ottoman and Venetian sources, but the Ottoman perspective is less well-represented in English-language historiography. Non-elite perspectives, women's experiences, and the impact on common people are underrepresented across all regions.

graph_edges.note: Birth of James Francis Edward Stuart precipitated the succession crisis leading to the Glorious Revolution
Glorious Revolution led to Battle of the Boyne
Glorious Revolution led to Bill of Rights
Williamite War in Ireland led to Battle of the Boyne
War of the League of Augsburg led to Treaty of Ryswick
Ayutthaya court coup led to expulsion of French from Siam
